summary: Gruber dissects a Wall Street Journal reporter's Apple Watch coverage, showing how a June 2014 story claiming the watch would have 'more than 10 sensors' and ship by year's end was contradicted by the actual product. When the reporter published a follow-up explaining that Apple had scaled back its health-monitoring ambitions, Gruber argues this reads less like genuine insider knowledge and more like retroactive justification for inaccurate reporting. He notes the timeline doesn't match how Apple actually develops products, and that the sources were likely working from outdated or incomplete information.
key_points: The WSJ's June 2014 report claimed Apple Watch would ship with more than 10 health sensors by year's end, but it shipped with only two
The follow-up story frames Apple as having scaled back ambitions, but Gruber argues the original reporting was simply wrong about the timeline and specs
Apple's actual product development process involves trying many ideas and narrowing focus over years, not last-minute sensor removal months before shipping
The timeline contradicts itself: Apple supposedly thought it was four months from shipping in June but didn't pre-announce until September, when it was seven months away
Gruber identifies a pattern where reporters reframe inaccurate rumors as evidence that the shipping product fell short rather than admitting the rumor was wrong
money_quotes: The artful dodge: the rumor was actually right; it's the shipping product that contradicts the rumor that is wrong.

In June they expected to ship a watch with more than 10 sensors by October, but by September they'd scrapped all those sensors other than the accelerometer and heart rate monitor and moved the shipping deadline back by six months.
The way it reads to me is that Wakabayashi's sources for the June 2014 story were not 'familiar with the matter', but rather were familiar with, at best, already-outdated plans.
That's how they develop products: come up with a slew of ideas, try the ideas that seem best, iterate and refine and change (narrowing focus) until they get to something that feels right.
His report this week reads more like an attempt to make it look like it's the Apple Watch that is actually coming in April that is wrong, not his reporting from last year.
key_insight: When tech reporters' predictions about Apple products prove wrong, some reframe the narrative so the shipping product looks like the failure rather than the rumor.
